parameters.text:
=[BEGIN SYSTEM]
{{ $('Get System Instructions').last().json.content }}
[END SYSTEM]

{{ $('Get Series Data').item.json.content }}
{{ $('Get Series Dossier').item.json.content }}

[BEGIN GENRE ANALYSES]
{{ $('Get Analyses').item.json.content }}
[END GENRE ANALYSES]

[BEGIN SUGGESTED PLOTS]
{{ $json.text }}
[END SUGGESTED PLOTS]

[BEGIN INSTRUCTIONS]
Evaluate the suggested plot outlines against the following criteria: 
Alignment with Market Trends: Does the plot reflect popular subgenres, tropes, and themes observed in the [analysis]?
Emotional Conflict and Stakes: Does the plot create strong, believable emotional conflict and escalate stakes effectively?
Character Chemistry: Do the protagonists have distinct, complementary personalities that generate sparks and tension?
Plot Pacing and Structure: Does the plot hit key storytelling beats (e.g., inciting incident, turning points, climax, resolution) and maintain tension throughout?
Unique Twist or Hook: Does the plot include a unique or fresh element that sets it apart from similar stories?
Romantic Payoff and HEA/HFN Satisfaction (if applicable): Does the ending feel earned and emotionally fulfilling, delivering on the expectations of the genre?
Marketability: Does the plot have an easily pitchable, high_concept element that makes it appealing to fans of the genre?

Assign a score between 1 and 10 for each criterion. 
1-3: Weak or poorly executed.
4-7: Average or functional, but not outstanding.
8-10: Excellent, compelling, and well-aligned with reader expectations.

Provide reasoning for each score. Be concise but specific. Explain why the score was assigned and identify strengths or weaknesses in the plot for that criterion.

Calculate the total score. Add up the individual scores for each criterion to produce a final total out of 70.

Summarize your findings. Highlight the plot's strengths, weaknesses, and overall potential for success.

Highlight the highest scoring suggested plot outline with a brief explanation of the market potential.

Based on the scores and evaluations, which plot stands out the most?
Explain why this plot is the best choice and suggest any final improvements.
[END INSTRUCTIONS]

=[BEGIN SYSTEM]
{{ $('Get System Instructions').item.json.content }}
[END SYSTEM]

{{ $json.content }}

[BEGIN INSTRUCTIONS]

Based on the [SELECTED PLOT], please create a list of 10 subplot options that would enrich the [SELECTED PLOT]. 

No incest or incest-adjacent romantic relationships, please! This includes distant cousins!

Evaluate each subplot based on: 
Thematic Relevance
Character Development
Plot Enhancement and Balance
Narrative Economy
Resolution and Payoff

Assign a score from 1 to 10 for each criterion.

Summarize your findings. Highlight the top 3 subplots' strengths, weaknesses, and overall potential for success in this story.
[END INSTRUCTIONS]

=[BEGIN SYSTEM]
{{ $('On form submission').last().json['System Instructions Document ID'] }}
[END SYSTEM]

{{ $json.content }}

[BEGIN INSTRUCTIONS]
We are creating a richly developed main character for the novel premise above, which will be written in deep POV. This character will be the PROTAGONIST of the two primary POV characters. 

Based on the inputs below, create a comprehensive character document that includes the following outputs:

1. Full Character Profile:
Use the format below to generate a detailed character sheet. Fill in each section thoughtfully and creatively. If details are missing, invent plausible ones that fit the tone and genre.

[BEGIN CHARACTER PROFILE TEMPLATE]
{{ $('Load MC Template').last().json.content }}
[END CHARACTER PROFILE TEMPLATE]

2. Emotional Wound & Identity Conflict:
Based on their personal history, flaws, regrets, fears, and formative moments, describe the character’s central emotional wound. Explain how this shapes their worldview, behavior, and interpersonal relationships at the start of the story. 

3. Internal Voice Sample:
Write a first-person internal monologue as the character reacts to a frustrating or mildly absurd situation (e.g., tech glitch, passive-aggressive comment, an awkward run-in). Use their voice to show tone, inner rhythm, filters, and emotional bias. Include sensory and emotional detail, per the author’s stylistic guidelines.

4. Growth in Action Scenelet:
Depending on book POV as indicated in the [STORY DATA], write a short third-person or first-person POV scene showing the character making a meaningful choice that reflects their personal growth. Contrast how they might have behaved earlier in the story with how they act now.

Style Requirements:
Follow the author’s writing style: deep emotional POV, sensory grounding, indirect emotional cues, layered interpersonal dynamics, and dry or understated humor. Avoid sentimentality and head-hopping. Adapt for genre if specified.

[END INSTRUCTIONS]

=[BEGIN SYSTEM]
{{ $('On form submission').last().json['System Instructions Document ID'] }}
[END SYSTEM]

{{ $('Get Story Data1').item.json.content }}
[BEGIN PROTAGONIST PROFILE]

[END PROTAGONIST PROFILE]

[BEGIN INSTRUCTIONS]
We are creating a richly developed main character for the novel premise above, which will be written in deep POV. This character will be the DEUTERAGONIST of the two primary POV characters. 

Based on the inputs below, create a comprehensive character document that includes the following outputs:

[BEGIN CHARACTER PROFILE TEMPLATE]
{{ $('Load MC Template').last().json.content }}
[END CHARACTER PROFILE TEMPLATE]

2. Emotional Wound & Identity Conflict:
Based on their personal history, flaws, regrets, fears, and formative moments, describe the character’s central emotional wound. Explain how this shapes their worldview, behavior, and interpersonal relationships at the start of the story. 

3. Internal Voice Sample:
Write a first-person internal monologue as the character reacts to a frustrating or mildly absurd situation (e.g., tech glitch, passive-aggressive comment, an awkward run-in). Use their voice to show tone, inner rhythm, filters, and emotional bias. Include sensory and emotional detail, per the author’s stylistic guidelines.

4. Growth in Action Scenelet:
Depending on book POV as indicated in the [STORY DATA], write a short third-person or first-person POV scene showing the character making a meaningful choice that reflects their personal growth. Contrast how they might have behaved earlier in the story with how they act now.

Style Requirements:
Follow the author’s writing style: deep emotional POV, sensory grounding, indirect emotional cues, layered interpersonal dynamics, and dry or understated humor. Avoid sentimentality and head-hopping. Adapt for genre if specified.

[END INSTRUCTIONS]

[BEGIN CHARACTER NAME DATABASE]
{{ $('Load Character Names DB').item.json.content }}
[BEGIN CHARACTER NAME DATABASE]

[BEGIN INSTRUCTIONS]
Please develop a roster of other supporting characters that would be appropriate for a project of this length. The list should include name, role in the story, and a brief description. Please select first and last names for each character from the [CHARACTER NAME DATABASE] provided.

Then, using the [TEMPLATE] below, please create a character profile for each secondary character.

[BEGIN TEMPLATE]
{{ $('Load SC Template').item.json.content }}
[END TEMPLATE]

No incest or incest-adjacent romantic relationships, please! This includes distant cousins!
[END INSTRUCTIONS]
